function.name: patch
function.description:
Targeted find-and-replace edits in files. Use this instead of sed/awk in terminal. Uses fuzzy matching (9 strategies) so minor whitespace/indentation differences won't break it. Returns a unified diff. Auto-runs syntax checks after editing.

Replace mode (default): find a unique string and replace it.
Patch mode: apply V4A multi-file patches for bulk changes.

mode.description: Edit mode: 'replace' for targeted find-and-replace, 'patch' for V4A multi-file patches

path.description: File path to edit (required for 'replace' mode)

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'replace' mode). Must be unique in the file unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'replace' mode). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: Replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false)

patch.description:
V4A format patch content (required for 'patch' mode). Format:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: path/to/file
@@ context hint @@
 context line
-removed line
+added line
*** End Patch

function.name: process
function.description: Manage background processes started with terminal(background=true). Actions: 'list' (show all), 'poll' (check status + new output), 'log' (full output with pagination), 'wait' (block until done or timeout), 'kill' (terminate), 'write' (send raw stdin data without newline), 'submit' (send data + Enter, for answering prompts).

action.description: Action to perform on background processes

session_id.description: Process session ID (from terminal background output). Required for all actions except 'list'.

data.description: Text to send to process stdin (for 'write' and 'submit' actions)

timeout.description: Max seconds to block for 'wait' action. Returns partial output on timeout.

offset.description: Line offset for 'log' action (default: last 200 lines)

limit.description: Max lines to return for 'log' action

function.name: read_file
function.description: Read a text file with line numbers and pagination. Use this instead of cat/head/tail in terminal. Output format: 'LINE_NUM|CONTENT'. Suggests similar filenames if not found. Use offset and limit for large files. Reads exceeding ~100K characters are rejected; use offset and limit to read specific sections of large files. NOTE: Cannot read images or binary files — use vision_analyze for images.

path.description: Path to the file to read (absolute, relative, or ~/path)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed, default: 1)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read (default: 500, max: 2000)

function.name: search_files
function.description:
Search file contents or find files by name. Use this instead of grep/rg/find/ls in terminal. Ripgrep-backed, faster than shell equivalents.

Content search (target='content'): Regex search inside files. Output modes: full matches with line numbers, file paths only, or match counts.

File search (target='files'): Find files by glob pattern (e.g., '*.py', '*config*'). Also use this instead of ls — results sorted by modification time.

pattern.description: Regex pattern for content search, or glob pattern (e.g., '*.py') for file search

target.description: 'content' searches inside file contents, 'files' searches for files by name

path.description: Directory or file to search in (default: current working directory)

file_glob.description: Filter files by pattern in grep mode (e.g., '*.py' to only search Python files)

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return (default: 50)

offset.description: Skip first N results for pagination (default: 0)

output_mode.description: Output format for grep mode: 'content' shows matching lines with line numbers, 'files_only' lists file paths, 'count' shows match counts per file

context.description: Number of context lines before and after each match (grep mode only)

function.name: terminal
function.description:
Execute shell commands on a Linux environment. Filesystem usually persists between calls.

Do NOT use cat/head/tail to read files — use read_file instead.
Do NOT use grep/rg/find to search — use search_files instead.
Do NOT use ls to list directories — use search_files(target='files') instead.
Do NOT use sed/awk to edit files — use patch instead.
Do NOT use echo/cat heredoc to create files — use write_file instead.
Reserve terminal for: builds, installs, git, processes, scripts, network, package managers, and anything that needs a shell.

Foreground (default): Commands return INSTANTLY when done, even if the timeout is high. Set timeout=300 for long builds/scripts — you'll still get the result in seconds if it's fast. Prefer foreground for short commands.
Background: Set background=true to get a session_id. Two patterns:
  (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers).
  (2) Long-running tasks with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working on other things and the system auto-notifies you when the task finishes. Great for test suites, builds, deployments, or anything that takes more than a minute.
Use process(action="poll") for progress checks, process(action="wait") to block until done.
Working directory: Use 'workdir' for per-command cwd.
PTY mode: Set pty=true for interactive CLI tools (Codex, Claude Code, Python REPL).

Do NOT use vim/nano/interactive tools without pty=true — they hang without a pseudo-terminal. Pipe git output to cat if it might page.
Important: cloud sandboxes may be cleaned up, idled out, or recreated between turns. Persistent filesystem means files can resume later; it does NOT guarantee a continuously running machine or surviving background processes. Use terminal sandboxes for task work, not durable hosting.

command.description: The command to execute on the VM

background.description: Run the command in the background. Two patterns: (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers). (2) Long-running tasks paired with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working and get notified when the task finishes. For short commands, prefer foreground with a generous timeout instead.

timeout.description: Max seconds to wait (default: 180). Returns INSTANTLY when command finishes — set high for long tasks, you won't wait unnecessarily.

workdir.description: Working directory for this command (absolute path). Defaults to the session working directory.

check_interval.description: Seconds between automatic status checks for background processes (gateway/messaging only, minimum 30). When set, I'll proactively report progress.

pty.description: Run in pseudo-terminal (PTY) mode for interactive CLI tools like Codex, Claude Code, or Python REPL. Only works with local and SSH backends. Default: false.

notify_on_complete.description: When true (and background=true), you'll be automatically notified when the process finishes — no polling needed. Use this for tasks that take a while (tests, builds, deployments) so you can keep working on other things in the meantime.

function.name: write_file
function.description: Write content to a file, completely replacing existing content. Use this instead of echo/cat heredoc in terminal. Creates parent directories automatically. OVERWRITES the entire file — use 'patch' for targeted edits.

path.description: Path to the file to write (will be created if it doesn't exist, overwritten if it does)

content.description: Complete content to write to the file

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `backend/`: FastAPI service. Main app code lives in `backend/app/` with API routes in `backend/app/api/`, data models in `backend/app/models/`, schemas in `backend/app/schemas/`, and service logic in `backend/app/services/`.
- `backend/migrations/`: Alembic migrations (`backend/migrations/versions/` for generated revisions).
- `backend/tests/`: pytest suite (`test_*.py` naming).
- `backend/templates/`: backend-shipped templates used by gateway flows.
- `frontend/`: Next.js app. Routes under `frontend/src/app/`, shared components under `frontend/src/components/`, utilities under `frontend/src/lib/`.
- `frontend/src/api/generated/`: generated API client; regenerate instead of editing by hand.
- `docs/`: contributor and operations docs (start at `docs/README.md`).

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `make setup`: install/sync backend and frontend dependencies.
- `make check`: closest CI parity run (lint, typecheck, tests/coverage, frontend build).
- `docker compose -f compose.yml --env-file .env up -d --build`: run full stack.
- Fast local loop:
  - `docker compose -f compose.yml --env-file .env up -d db`
  - `cd backend && uv run uvicorn app.main:app --reload --port 8000`
  - `cd frontend && npm run dev`
- `make api-gen`: regenerate frontend API client (backend must be on `127.0.0.1:8000`).

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Python: Black + isort + flake8 + strict mypy. Max line length is 100. Use `snake_case`.
- TypeScript/React: ESLint + Prettier. Components use `PascalCase`; variables/functions use `camelCase`.
- For intentionally unused destructured TS variables, prefix with `_` to satisfy lint config.

## Testing Guidelines
- Backend: pytest via `make backend-test`; coverage policy via `make backend-coverage` (writes `backend/coverage.xml` and `backend/coverage.json`).
- Frontend: vitest + Testing Library via `make frontend-test` (coverage in `frontend/coverage/`).
- Add or update tests whenever behavior changes.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Follow Conventional Commits (seen in history), e.g. `feat: ...`, `fix: ...`, `docs: ...`, `test(core): ...`.
- Keep PRs focused and based on latest `master`.
- Include: what changed, why, test evidence (`make check` or targeted commands), linked issue, and screenshots/logs when UI or operator workflow changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Never commit secrets. Copy from `.env.example` and keep real values in local `.env`.
- Report vulnerabilities privately via GitHub security advisories, not public issues.

orchestration. 6|It gives operators a single interface for work orchestration, agent and gateway management, approval-driven governance, and API-backed automation. 7| 8| 9| 10| 11| 12| 13| 14|## Platform overview 15| 16|Mission Control is designed to be the day-to-day operations surface for OpenClaw. 17|Instead of splitting work across multiple tools, teams can plan, execute, review, and audit activity in one system. 18| 19|Core operational areas: 20| 21|- Work orchestration: manage organizations, board groups, boards, tasks, and tags. 22|- Agent operations: create, inspect, and manage agent lifecycle from a unified control surface. 23|- Governance and approvals: route sensitive actions through explicit approval flows. 24|- Gateway management: connect and operate gateway integrations for distributed environments. 25|- Activity visibility: review a timeline of system actions for faster debugging and accountability. 26|- API-first model: support both web workflows and automation clients from the same platform. 27| 28|## Use cases 29| 30|- Multi-team agent operations: run multiple boards and board groups across organizations from a single control plane. 31|- Human-in-the-loop execution: require approvals before sensitive actions and keep decision trails attached to work. 32|- Distributed runtime control: connect gateways and operate remote execution environments without changing operator workflow. 33|- Audit and incident review: use activity history to reconstruct what happened, when it happened, and who initiated it. 34|- API-backed process integration: connect internal workflows and automation clients to the same operational model used in the UI. 35| 36|## What makes Mission Control different 37| 38|- Operations-first design: built for running agent work reliably, not just creating tasks. 39|- Governance built in: approvals, auth modes, and clear control boundaries are first-class. 40|- Gateway-aware orchestration: built to operate both local and connected runtime environments. 41|- Unified UI and API model: operators and automation act on the same objects and lifecycle. 42|- Team-scale structure: organizations, board groups, boards, tasks, tags, and users in one system of record. 43| 44|## Who it is for 45| 46|- Platform teams running OpenClaw in self-hosted or internal environments. 47|- Operations and engineering teams that need clear approval and auditability controls. 48|- Organizations that want API-accessible operations without losing a usable web UI. 49| 50|## Get started in minutes 51| 52|### Option A: One-command production-style bootstrap 53| 54|If you haven't cloned the repo yet, you can run the installer in one line: 55| 56|```bash 57|curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/abhi1693/openclaw-mission-control/master/install.sh | bash 58|``` 59| 60|This clones the repository into `./openclaw-mission-control` if no local checkout is found in your current directory. 61| 62|If you already cloned the repo: 63| 64|```bash 65|./install.sh 66|``` 67| 68|The installer is interactive and will: 69| 70|- Ask for deployment mode (`docker` or `local`). 71|- Install missing system dependencies when possible. 72|- Generate and configure environment files. 73|- Bootstrap and start the selected deployment mode. 74| 75|Installer support matrix: On macOS, Docker mode requires [Docker Desktop](https://www.docker.com/products/docker-desktop/); local mode requires [Homebrew](https://brew.sh) and Node.js 22+. 82|- Docker Engine 83|- Docker Compose v2 (`docker compose`) 84| 85|### 1. Configure environment 86| 87|```bash 88|cp .env.example .env 89|``` 90| 91|Before startup: 92| 93|- Set `LOCAL_AUTH_TOKEN` to a non-placeholder value (minimum 50 characters) when `AUTH_MODE=*** 94|- Ensure `BASE_URL` matches the public backend origin if you are not using `http://localhost:8000`. 95|- `NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL=auto` (default) resolves to `http(s)://:8000`. 96| - Set an explicit URL when your API is behind a reverse proxy or non-default port. 97| 98|### 2. Start Mission Control 99| 100|```bash 101|docker compose -f compose.yml --env-file .env up -d --build 102|``` 103| 104|If you are iterating on the UI in Docker and want automatic frontend rebuilds on 105|source changes, run: 106| 107|```bash 108|docker compose -f compose.yml --env-file .env up --build --watch 109|``` 110| 111|Notes: 112| 113|- Compose Watch requires Docker Compose **2.22.0+**. 114|- You can also run watch separately after startup: 115| 116|```bash 117|docker compose -f compose.yml --env-file .env up -d --build 118|docker compose -f compose.yml --env-file .env watch 119|``` 120| 121|After pulling new changes, rebuild and recreate all services: 122| 123|```bash 124|docker compose -f compose.yml --env-file .env up -d --build --force-recreate 125|``` 126| 127|For a fully clean rebuild (no cached build layers): 128| 129|```bash 130|docker compose -f compose.yml --env-file .env build --no-cache --pull 131|docker compose -f compose.yml --env-file .env up -d --force-recreate 132|``` 133| 134|### 3. {"content": "     1|# API reference (notes + conventions)
     2|
     3|Mission Control exposes a JSON HTTP API (FastAPI) under `/api/v1/*`.
     4|
     5|- Default backend base URL (local): `http://localhost:8000`
     6|- Health endpoints:
     7|  - `GET /health` (liveness)
     8|  - `GET /healthz` (liveness alias)
     9|  - `GET /readyz` (readiness)
    10|
    11|## OpenAPI / Swagger
    12|
    13|- OpenAPI schema: `GET /openapi.json`
    14|- Swagger UI (FastAPI default): `GET /docs`
    15|
    16|> If you are building clients, prefer generating from `openapi.json`.
    17|
    18|## API versioning
    19|
    20|- Current prefix: `/api/v1`
    21|- Backwards compatibility is **best-effort** while the project is under active development.
    22|
    23|## Authentication
    24|
    25|All protected endpoints expect a bearer token:
    26|
    27|```http
    28|Authorization: Bearer ***
    29|```
    30|
    31|Auth mode is controlled by `AUTH_MODE`:
    32|
    33|- `local`: shared bearer token auth (token is `LOCAL_AUTH_TOKEN`)
    34|- `clerk`: Clerk JWT auth
    35|
    36|Notes:
    37|- The frontend uses the same bearer token scheme in local mode (users paste the token into the UI).
    38|- Many “agent” endpoints use an agent token header instead (see below).
    39|
    40|### Agent auth (Mission Control agents)
    41|
    42|Some endpoints are designed for autonomous agents and use an agent token header:
    43|
    44|```http
    45|X-Agent-Token: <agent-token>
    46|```
    47|
    48|On shared user/agent routes, the backend also accepts `Authorization: Bearer *** after user auth does not resolve. When in doubt, consult the route’s dependencies (e.g., `require_user_or_agent`).
    49|
    50|Agent authentication is rate-limited to **20 requests per 60 seconds per IP**. Exceeding this limit returns `429 Too Many Requests`.
    51|
    52|## Authorization / permissions model (high level)
    53|
    54|The backend distinguishes between:
    55|
    56|- **users** (humans) authenticated via `AUTH_MODE`
    57|- **agents** authenticated via agent tokens
    58|
    59|Common patterns:
    60|
    61|- **User-only** endpoints: require an authenticated human user (not an agent). Organization-level admin checks are enforced separately where needed (`require_org_admin`).
    62|- **User or agent** endpoints: allow either an authenticated human user or an authenticated agent.
    63|- **Board-scoped access**: user/agent access may be restricted to a specific board.
    64|
    65|> SOC2 note: the API produces an audit-friendly request id (see below), but role/permission policy should be documented per endpoint as we stabilize.
    66|
    67|## Security headers
    68|
    69|All API responses include the following security headers by default:
    70|
    71|| Header | Default |
    72|| --- | --- |
    73|| `X-Content-Type-Options` | `nosniff` |
    74|| `X-Frame-Options` | `DENY` |
    75|| `Referrer-Policy` | `strict-origin-when-cross-origin` |
    76|| `Permissions-Policy` | _(disabled)_ |
    77|
    78|Each header is configurable via `SECURITY_HEADER_*` environment variables. Set a variable to blank to disable the corresponding header (see [configuration reference](configuration.md)).
    79|
    80|## Rate limits
    81|
    82|The following per-IP rate limits are enforced on sensitive endpoints:
    83|
    84|| Endpoint | Limit | Window |
    85|| --- | --- | --- |
    86|| Agent authentication (`X-Agent-Token` or agent bearer fallback on shared routes) | 20 requests | 60 seconds |
    87|| Webhook ingest (`POST .../webhooks/{id}`) | 60 requests | 60 seconds |
    88|
    89|When a rate limit is exceeded, the API returns `429 Too Many Requests`.
    90|
    91|Set `RATE_LIMIT_BACKEND` to choose the storage backend:
    92|
    93|| Backend | Value | Behavior |
    94|| --- | --- | --- |
    95|| In-memory (default) | `memory` | Per-process limits; no external dependencies. |
    96|| Redis | `redis` | Shared across all workers. Set `RATE_LIMIT_REDIS_URL` or it falls back to `RQ_REDIS_URL`. Connectivity is validated at startup; transient failures fail open. |
    97|
    98|> **Note:** When using the in-memory backend, limits are per-process. Multi-process deployments should either switch to the Redis backend or apply rate limiting at the reverse proxy layer (nginx `limit_req`, Caddy, etc.).
    99|
   100|## Request IDs
   101|
   102|Every response includes an `X-Request-Id` header.
   103|
   104|- Clients may supply their own `X-Request-Id`; otherwise the server generates one.
   105|- Use this id to correlate client reports with server logs.
   106|
   107|## Errors
   108|
   109|Errors are returned as JSON with a stable top-level shape:
   110|
   111|```json
   112|{
   113|  "detail": "...",
   114|  "request_id": "..."
   115|}
   116|```
   117|
   118|Common status codes:
   119|
   120|- `401 Unauthorized`: missing/invalid credentials
   121|- `403 Forbidden`: authenticated but not allowed
   122|- `404 Not Found`: resource missing (or not visible)
   123|- `413 Content Too Large`: request payload exceeds size limit (e.g. webhook ingest 1 MB cap)
   124|- `422 Unprocessable Entity`: request validation error
   125|- `429 Too Many Requests`: per-IP rate limit exceeded
   126|- `500 Internal Server Error`: unhandled server errors
   127|
   128|Validation errors (`422`) typically return `detail` as a list of structured field errors (FastAPI/Pydantic style).
   129|
   130|## Pagination
   131|
   132|List endpoints commonly return an `items` array with paging fields (varies by endpoint). If you’re implementing new list endpoints, prefer consistent parameters:
   133|
   134|- `limit`
   135|- `offset`
   136|
   137|…and return:
   138|
   139|- `items: []`
   140|- `total`
   141|- `limit`
   142|- `offset`
   143|
   144|## Examples (curl)
   145|
   146|### Health
   147|
   148|```bash
   149|curl -f http://localhost:8000/healthz
   150|```
   151|
   152|### Agent heartbeat check-in
   153|
   154|```bash
   155|curl -s -X POST http://localhost:8000/api/v1/agent/heartbeat \
   156|  -H "X-Agent-Token: $AUTH_TOKEN" \
   157|  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
   158|  -d '{"name":"Tessa","board_id":"<board-id>","status":"online"}'
   159|```
   160|
   161|### List tasks for a board
   162|
   163|```bash
   164|curl -s "http://localhost:8000/api/v1/agent/boards/<board-id>/tasks?status=inbox&limit=10" \
   165|  -H "X-Agent-Token: $AUTH_TOKEN"
   166|```
   167|
   168|## Gaps / follow-ups
   169|
   170|- Per-endpoint documentation of:
   171|  - required auth header (`Authorization` vs `X-Agent-Token`)
   172|  - required role (admin vs member vs agent)
   173|  - common error responses per endpoint
   174|- Rate limits are documented above; consider exposing them via OpenAPI `x-ratelimit-*` extensions.
   175|- Add canonical examples for:
   176|  - creating/updating tasks + comments
   177|  - board memory streaming
   178|  - approvals workflow
   179|", "total_lines": 178, "file_size": 5791, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

     5|- `local`: shared bearer token auth for self-hosted deployments
     6|- `clerk`: Clerk JWT auth
     7|
     8|## Local mode
     9|

    19|

    24|- `AUTH_MODE=***

    26|
    27|Frontend:
    28|
    29|- `NEXT_PUBLIC_AUTH_MODE=***
    30|- `NEXT_PUBLIC_CLERK_PUBLISHABLE_KEY=<key>`
    31|
    32|## Agent authentication
    33|
    34|Autonomous agents primarily authenticate via an `X-Agent-Token` header. On shared user/agent routes, the backend also accepts `Authorization: Bearer *** after user auth does not resolve. See [API reference](api.md) for details.
    35|
    36|Security notes:
    37|

{"content": "     1|# Configuration reference
     2|
     3|This page collects the most important config values.
     4|
     5|## Root `.env` (Compose)
     6|
     7|See `.env.example` for defaults and required values.
     8|
     9|### `NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL`
    10|
    11|- **Where set:** `.env` (frontend container environment)
    12|- **Purpose:** Public URL the browser uses to call the backend.
    13|- **Gotcha:** Must be reachable from the *browser* (host), not a Docker network alias.
    14|
    15|### `LOCAL_AUTH_TOKEN`
    16|
    17|- **Where set:** `.env` (backend)
    18|- **When required:** `AUTH_MODE=***
    19|- **Policy:** Must be non-placeholder and at least 50 characters.
    20|
    21|### `WEBHOOK_MAX_PAYLOAD_BYTES`
    22|
    23|- **Default:** `1048576` (1 MiB)
    24|- **Purpose:** Maximum accepted inbound webhook payload size before the API returns `413 Content Too Large`.
    25|
    26|### `RATE_LIMIT_BACKEND`
    27|
    28|- **Default:** `memory`
    29|- **Allowed values:** `memory`, `redis`
    30|- **Purpose:** Selects whether rate limits are tracked per-process in memory or shared through Redis.
    31|
    32|### `RATE_LIMIT_REDIS_URL`
    33|
    34|- **Default:** _(blank)_
    35|- **When required:** `RATE_LIMIT_BACKEND=redis` and `RQ_REDIS_URL` is not set
    36|- **Purpose:** Redis connection string used for shared rate limits.
    37|- **Fallback:** If blank and Redis rate limiting is enabled, the backend falls back to `RQ_REDIS_URL`.
    38|
    39|### `TRUSTED_PROXIES`
    40|
    41|- **Default:** _(blank)_
    42|- **Purpose:** Comma-separated list of trusted reverse-proxy IPs or CIDRs used to honor `Forwarded` / `X-Forwarded-For` client IP headers.
    43|- **Gotcha:** Leave this blank unless the direct peer is a proxy you control.
    44|
    45|## Security response headers
    46|
    47|These environment variables control security headers added to every API response. Set any variable to blank (`""`) to disable the corresponding header.
    48|
    49|### `SECURITY_HEADER_X_CONTENT_TYPE_OPTIONS`
    50|
    51|- **Default:** `nosniff`
    52|- **Purpose:** Prevents browsers from MIME-type sniffing responses.
    53|
    54|### `SECURITY_HEADER_X_FRAME_OPTIONS`
    55|
    56|- **Default:** `DENY`
    57|- **Purpose:** Prevents the API from being embedded in iframes.
    58|- **Note:** If your deployment embeds the API in an iframe, set this to `SAMEORIGIN` or blank.
    59|
    60|### `SECURITY_HEADER_REFERRER_POLICY`
    61|
    62|- **Default:** `strict-origin-when-cross-origin`
    63|- **Purpose:** Controls how much referrer information is sent with requests.
    64|
    65|### `SECURITY_HEADER_PERMISSIONS_POLICY`
    66|
    67|- **Default:** _(blank — disabled)_
    68|- **Purpose:** Restricts browser features (camera, microphone, etc.) when set.
    69|", "total_lines": 68, "file_size": 2368, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

   174|def _truncate_preview(raw: str, max_chars: int) -> str:
   175|    if len(raw) <= max_chars:
   176|        return raw
   177|    if max_chars <= 3:
   178|        return raw[:max_chars]
   179|    return f"{raw[: max_chars - 3]}..."

   182|def _payload_preview_with_limit(
   183|    value: dict[str, object] | list[object] | str | int | float | bool | None,
   184|    *,
   185|    max_chars: int,
   186|) -> tuple[str, bool]:
   187|    if isinstance(value, str):
   188|        return _truncate_preview(value, max_chars), len(value) > max_chars
   189|
   190|    try:
   191|        # Stream JSON chunks so we can stop once we know truncation is required.
   192|        encoder = json.JSONEncoder(ensure_ascii=True)
   193|        parts: list[str] = []
   194|        current_len = 0
   195|        truncated = False
   196|        for chunk in encoder.iterencode(value):
   197|            remaining = (max_chars + 1) - current_len
   198|            if remaining <= 0:
   199|                truncated = True
   200|                break
   201|            if len(chunk) <= remaining:
   202|                parts.append(chunk)
   203|                current_len += len(chunk)
   204|                continue
   205|            parts.append(chunk[:remaining])
   206|            current_len += remaining
   207|            truncated = True
   208|            break
   209|        raw = "".join(parts)
   210|    except TypeError:
   211|        raw = str(value)
   212|        return _truncate_preview(raw, max_chars), len(raw) > max_chars
   213|
   214|    if len(raw) > max_chars:
   215|        truncated = True
   216|    if not truncated:
   217|        return raw, False
   218|    return _truncate_preview(raw, max_chars), True

     2|
     3|from __future__ import annotations
     4|
     5|import base64
     6|import hashlib
     7|import hmac
     8|import secrets
     9|
    10|ITERATIONS = 200_000
    11|SALT_BYTES = 16
    12|
    13|
    14|def generate_agent_token() -> str:
    15|    """Generate a new URL-safe random token for an agent."""
    16|    return secrets.token_urlsafe(32)
    17|
    18|
    19|def _b64encode(value: bytes) -> str:
    20|    return base64.urlsafe_b64encode(value).decode("utf-8").rstrip("=")
    21|
    22|
    23|def _b64decode(value: str) -> bytes:
    24|    padding = "=" * (-len(value) % 4)
    25|    return base64.urlsafe_b64decode(value + padding)
    26|
    27|
    28|def hash_agent_token(token: str) -> str:
    29|    """Hash an agent token using PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 with a random salt."""
    30|    salt = secrets.token_bytes(SALT_BYTES)
    31|    digest = hashlib.pbkdf2_hmac("sha256", token.encode("utf-8"), salt, ITERATIONS)
    32|    return f"pbkdf2_sha256${ITERATIONS}${_b64encode(salt)}${_b64encode(digest)}"
    33|
    34|
    35|def verify_agent_token(token: str, stored_hash: str) -> bool:
    36|    """Verify a plaintext token against a stored PBKDF2 hash representation."""
    37|    try:
    38|        algorithm, iterations, salt_b64, digest_b64 = stored_hash.split("$")
    39|    except ValueError:
    40|        return False
    41|    if algorithm != "pbkdf2_sha256":
    42|        return False
    43|    try:
    44|        iterations_int = int(iterations)
    45|    except ValueError:
    46|        return False
    47|    salt = _b64decode(salt_b64)
    48|    expected_digest = _b64decode(digest_b64)
    49|    candidate = hashlib.pbkdf2_hmac(
    50|        "sha256",
    51|        token.encode("utf-8"),
    52|        salt,
    53|        iterations_int,
    54|    )
    55|    return hmac.compare_digest(candidate, expected_digest)

    40|class AgentLifecycleOrchestrator(OpenClawDBService):
    41|    """Single lifecycle writer for agent provision/update transitions."""
    42|
    43|    def __init__(self, session: AsyncSession) -> None:
    44|        super().__init__(session)

    46|    async def _lock_agent(self, *, agent_id: UUID) -> Agent:
    47|        statement = select(Agent).where(col(Agent.id) == agent_id).with_for_update()
    48|        agent = (await self.session.exec(statement)).first()
    49|        if agent is None:
    50|            raise HTTPException(status_code=status.HTTP_404_NOT_FOUND, detail="Agent not found")
    51|        return agent

    53|    async def run_lifecycle(
    54|        self,
    55|        *,
    56|        gateway: Gateway,
    57|        agent_id: UUID,
    58|        board: Board | None,

    62|        force_bootstrap: bool = False,
    63|        reset_session: bool = False,
    64|        wake: bool = True,

    66|        wakeup_verb: str | None = None,
    67|        clear_confirm_token: bool = False,
    68|        raise_gateway_errors: bool = True,
    69|    ) -> Agent:
    70|        """Provision or update any agent under a per-agent lock."""
    71|
    72|        locked = await self._lock_agent(agent_id=agent_id)
    73|        template_user = user
    74|        if board is None and template_user is None:
    75|            template_user = await get_org_owner_user(

    85|                    ),

    87|
    88|        raw_token = auth_token or mint_agent_token(locked)
    89|        mark_provision_requested(

    96|        locked.checkin_deadline_at = utcnow() + CHECKIN_DEADLINE_AFTER_WAKE if wake else None
    97|        if wake:

   170|## Section-by-Section Reference
   171|
   172|This is what each section controls and why you would tune it.
   173|
   174|### `env`
   175|
   176|Controls runtime environment behavior.
   177|
   178|- `env.shellEnv.enabled`: when `true`, OpenClaw can resolve environment from shell context, which helps tools and model/provider discovery behave consistently with your shell session.
   179|
   180|Operational note:
   181|
   182|- If shell startup is heavy or slow, consider also setting `env.shellEnv.timeoutMs` (optional key supported by schema) to cap lookup time.
   183|
   184|### `update`
   185|
   186|Controls update policy for npm/git installs.
   187|
   188|- `update.channel`: release track (`stable`, `beta`, `dev`).
   189|
   190|Recommended baseline:
   191|
   192|- `stable` for production-ish use.
   193|- Use `beta`/`dev` only when you actively want pre-release behavior.
   194|
   195|### `agents`
   196|
   197|Defines default agent runtime behavior plus agent list.
   198|
   199|#### `agents.defaults.model`
   200|
   201|Model routing defaults.
   202|
   203|- `primary`: main model id for agent turns.
   204|- `fallbacks`: ordered backup model ids used when primary fails.
   205|
   206|Important:
   207|
   208|- Empty `primary` means no explicit default model is selected.
   209|- Set this before first real use.
   210|
   211|#### `agents.defaults.models`
   212|
   213|Per-model override map keyed by full model id.
   214|
   215|- In your baseline, key is `""`; replace this with a real model id.
   216|- Value object can hold per-model params in supported versions.
   217|
   218|#### `agents.defaults.workspace`
   219|
   220|Filesystem root for agent state/workspaces.
   221|
   222|- Must exist and be writable by the runtime.
   223|- Align this with Mission Control gateway `workspace_root` for consistency.
   224|
   225|#### `agents.defaults.contextPruning`
   226|
   227|Controls prompt-history tool-output pruning to keep context size healthy.
   228|
   229|- `mode: "cache-ttl"`: enables pruning extension with TTL-aware behavior.
   230|- `ttl`: minimum time before pruning runs again (example `45m`).
   231|- `keepLastAssistants`: protects recent assistant turns from pruning cutoff.
   232|- `minPrunableToolChars`: only hard-clear when prunable tool output is large enough.
   233|- `tools.deny`: tool names excluded from pruning.
   234|- `softTrim`: partial shortening of tool output.
   235|- `hardClear`: full replacement with placeholder when limits are exceeded.
   236|
   237|Practical effect:
   238|
   239|- `softTrim` keeps beginning/end context for long outputs.
   240|- `hardClear` prevents repeated old tool dumps from consuming context.
   241|
   242|#### `agents.defaults.compaction`
   243|
   244|Controls how conversation history is compacted and protected against token overflow.
   245|
   246|- `mode: "safeguard"`: conservative compaction strategy.
   247|- `reserveTokensFloor`: hard reserve to avoid running context to exhaustion.
   248|- `memoryFlush`: pre-compaction memory checkpoint behavior.
   249|
   250|`memoryFlush` keys:
   251|
   252|- `enabled`: turn memory flush on/off.
   253|- `softThresholdTokens`: triggers flush before compaction line is crossed.
   254|- `prompt`: user-prompt text for flush turn.
   255|- `systemPrompt`: system instruction for flush turn.
   256|
   257|What this protects:
   258|
   259|- Avoids losing durable context when sessions approach compaction.
   260|
   261|#### `agents.defaults.thinkingDefault`
   262|
   263|Default reasoning intensity for turns.
   264|
   265|- Your baseline uses `medium` as a quality/speed balance.
   266|
   267|#### Concurrency Controls
   268|
   269|- `agents.defaults.maxConcurrent`: max parallel top-level runs.
   270|- `agents.defaults.subagents.maxConcurrent`: max parallel subagent runs.
   271|
   272|Use these to control throughput versus host/API pressure.
   273|
   274|#### `agents.list`
   275|
   276|Defines configured agents.
   277|
   278|- `[{ "id": "main" }]` creates the primary default agent identity.
   279|
   280|### `messages`
   281|
   282|Inbound/outbound messaging behavior.
   283|
   284|- `messages.ackReactionScope`: where ack reactions are emitted.
   285|
   286|Allowed values:
   287|
   288|- `group-mentions`, `group-all`, `direct`, `all`
   289|
   290|Baseline intent:
   291|
   292|- `group-mentions` avoids noisy acks in busy group channels.
   293|
   294|### `commands`
   295|
   296|Native command registration behavior for supported channels.
   297|
   298|- `commands.native`: command registration mode (`true`/`false`/`auto`).
   299|- `commands.nativeSkills`: skill command registration mode (`true`/`false`/`auto`).
   300|
   301|Baseline intent:
   302|
   303|- `auto` lets OpenClaw decide based on channel/provider capabilities.
   304|
   305|### `hooks`
   306|
   307|Internal hook system settings.
   308|
   309|- `hooks.internal.enabled`: turns internal hooks system on/off.
   310|- `hooks.internal.entries`: per-hook enable/config map.
   311|
   312|Your baseline entries:
   313|
   314|- `boot-md`: runs BOOT.md startup checklist hook.
   315|- `command-logger`: writes command audit logs.
   316|- `session-memory`: stores context when `/new` is used.
   317|- `bootstrap-extra-files`: custom/optional hook id.
   318|
   319|Important:
   320|
   321|- Hook IDs not installed on the runtime are ignored or reported missing.
   322|- Verify available hooks with `openclaw hooks list`.
   323|
   324|### `channels`
   325|
   326|Cross-channel defaults.
   327|
   328|#### `channels.defaults.heartbeat`
   329|
   330|Controls heartbeat visibility behavior (global default layer).
   331|
   332|- `showOk`: emit explicit OK heartbeat messages.
   333|- `showAlerts`: emit non-OK/alert heartbeat content.
   334|- `useIndicator`: emit indicator events alongside heartbeat behavior.
   335|
   336|Baseline intent:
   337|
   338|- Everything on (`true`) gives explicit operational visibility.
   339|
   340|### `gateway`
   341|
   342|Core gateway server behavior.
   343|
   344|#### Network & Mode
   345|
   346|- `port`: gateway WebSocket port.
   347|- `mode`: `local` or `remote` behavior mode.
   348|- `bind`: exposure strategy (`loopback`, `lan`, `tailnet`, `auto`, `custom`).
   349|
   350|Baseline choice:
   351|
   352|- `bind: "lan"` makes gateway reachable on local network interfaces.
   353|
   354|#### Control UI Security
   355|
   356|- `controlUi.allowInsecureAuth: true` allows token-only auth over insecure HTTP.
   357|
   358|Security implication:
   359|
   360|- Good for local development convenience.
   361|- Not recommended for exposed environments.
   362|
   363|#### Auth
   364|
   365|- `gateway.auth.mode`: `token` or `password`.
   366|- With `token` mode, set `gateway.auth.token` (or provide via env/CLI override) before non-local usage.
   367|
   368|#### Reverse Proxy Awareness
   369|
   370|- `gateway.trustedProxies`: proxy IP allowlist used for client IP/local detection behind reverse proxies.
   371|
   372|Why it matters:
   373|
   374|- Prevents false local-trust behavior when proxied traffic is present.
   375|
   376|#### Tailscale
   377|
   378|- `gateway.tailscale.mode`: `off`, `serve`, or `funnel`.
   379|- `resetOnExit`: whether to revert serve/funnel wiring on shutdown.
   380|
   381|#### Config Reload
   382|
   383|- `gateway.reload.mode`: reload strategy (`off`, `restart`, `hot`, `hybrid`).
   384|- `gateway.reload.debounceMs`: debounce before applying config changes.
   385|
   386|#### Node Command Policy
   387|
   388|- `gateway.nodes.denyCommands`: hard denylist for node-exposed commands.
   389|
   390|Baseline intent:
   391|
   392|- Blocks risky device/system actions from remote node invocations.
   393|
   394|### `memory`
   395|
   396|`memory` in your baseline appears to be plugin-style configuration (for `qmd`).
   397|
   398|Compatibility warning:
   399|
   400|- In OpenClaw `2026.1.30` core schema, top-level `memory` is not a built-in key.
   401|- Without a plugin that extends schema for this section, config validation reports:
   402|  `Unrecognized key: "memory"`.
   403|
   404|What to do:
   405|
   406|1. If you use a plugin that defines this block, keep it and validate with your plugin set.
   407|2. If not, remove this block and use core `agents.defaults.memorySearch` + plugin slots/entries for memory behavior.
   408|
   409|### `skills`
   410|
   411|Skill install/runtime behavior.
   412|
   413|- `skills.install.nodeManager`: package manager used for skill installation workflows.
   414|
   415|Allowed values:
   416|
   417|- `npm`, `pnpm`, `yarn`, `bun`
   418|
   419|Baseline choice:
   420|
   421|- `npm` for highest compatibility.
   422|
   423|## Validation Before Use
   424|
   425|Do a schema check before running production workloads:
   426|
   427|```bash
   428|openclaw config get gateway.port
   429|```
   430|
   431|If invalid, OpenClaw reports exact keys/paths and remediation.
   432|
   433|## Required Edits Before First Run
   434|
   435|These fields should be set before using this in production-like workflows:
   436|
   437|1. `agents.defaults.model.primary`
   438|   Set a concrete model id, for example `openai-codex/gpt-5.2`.
   439|2. `agents.defaults.models`
   440|   Replace the empty key (`""`) with your model id so per-model config is mapped correctly.
   441|3. `gateway.auth`
   442|   If token auth is enabled, provide the token value (for example `gateway.auth.token`) via your preferred secret handling approach.
   443|4. `memory` (top-level)
   444|   Keep only if your runtime/plugin set supports it. Otherwise remove to pass core schema validation.
   445|
   446|## Quick Start
   447|
   448|1. Create the config file:
   449|
   450|```bash
   451|mkdir -p ~/.openclaw
   452|```
   453|
   454|2. Save the JSON above to:
   455|
   456|- `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json`
   457|
   458|3. Start the gateway:
   459|
   460|```bash
   461|openclaw gateway
   462|```
   463|
   464|4. Verify health:
   465|
   466|```bash
   467|openclaw health
   468|```
   469|
   470|5. Open the control UI:
   471|
   472|```bash
   473|openclaw dashboard
   474|```
   475|
   476|## Mission Control Connection (This Repo)
   477|
   478|When adding a gateway in Mission Control:
   479|
   480|- URL: `ws://127.0.0.1:18789` (or your host/IP with explicit port)
   481|- Token: provide only if your gateway requires token auth
   482|- Device pairing: enabled by default and recommended
   483|  - Keep pairing enabled for normal operation.
   484|  - Optional bypass: enable `Disable device pairing` per gateway only when the gateway is explicitly configured for control UI auth bypass (for example `gateway.controlUi.dangerouslyDisableDeviceAuth: true` plus appropriate `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins`).
   485|- Workspace root (in Mission Control gateway config): align with `agents.defaults.workspace` when possible
   486|
   487|## Security Notes
   488|
   489|- `gateway.bind: "lan"` exposes the gateway on your local network.
   490|- `controlUi.allowInsecureAuth: true` is development-friendly and not recommended for exposed environments.
   491|- Use a strong token if `gateway.auth.mode` is `token`.
   492|
   493|## Why This Baseline Works
   494|
   495|- Sensible concurrency defaults for both primary and subagents.
   496|- Context-pruning + compaction settings tuned to reduce context bloat.
   497|- Memory flush before compaction to preserve durable notes.
   498|- Conservative command deny-list for risky node capabilities.

{"content": "     1|# Gateway Agent Provisioning and Check-In Troubleshooting
     2|
     3|This guide explains how agent provisioning converges to a healthy state, and how to debug when an agent appears stuck.
     4|
     5|## Fast Convergence Policy
     6|
     7|Mission Control now uses a fast convergence policy for wake/check-in:
     8|
     9|- Check-in deadline after each wake: **30 seconds**
    10|- Maximum wake attempts without check-in: **3**
    11|- If no check-in after the third attempt: agent is marked **offline** and provisioning escalation stops
    12|
    13|This applies to both gateway-main and board agents.
    14|
    15|## Expected Lifecycle
    16|
    17|1. Mission Control provisions/updates the agent and sends wake.
    18|2. A delayed reconcile task is queued for the check-in deadline.
    19|3. Agent should call heartbeat quickly after startup/bootstrap.
    20|4. If heartbeat arrives:
    21|   - `last_seen_at` is updated
    22|   - wake escalation state is reset (`wake_attempts=0`, check-in deadline cleared)
    23|5. If heartbeat does not arrive by deadline:
    24|   - reconcile re-runs lifecycle (wake again)
    25|   - up to 3 total wake attempts
    26|6. If still no heartbeat after 3 attempts:
    27|   - agent status becomes `offline`
    28|   - `last_provision_error` is set
    29|
    30|## Startup Check-In Behavior
    31|
    32|Templates now explicitly require immediate first-cycle check-in:
    33|
    34|- Main agent heartbeat instructions require immediate check-in after wake/bootstrap.
    35|- Board lead bootstrap requires heartbeat check-in before orchestration.
    36|- Board worker bootstrap already included immediate check-in.
    37|
    38|If a gateway still has older templates, run template sync and reprovision/wake.
    39|
    40|## What You Should See in Logs
    41|
    42|Healthy flow usually includes:
    43|
    44|- `lifecycle.queue.enqueued`
    45|- `queue.worker.success` (for lifecycle tasks)
    46|- `lifecycle.reconcile.skip_not_stuck` (after heartbeat lands)
    47|
    48|If agent is not checking in:
    49|
    50|- `lifecycle.reconcile.deferred` (before deadline)
    51|- `lifecycle.reconcile.retriggered` (retry wake)
    52|- `lifecycle.reconcile.max_attempts_reached` (final fail-safe at attempt 3)
    53|
    54|If you do not see lifecycle events at all, verify queue worker health first.
    55|
    56|## Common Failure Modes
    57|
    58|### Wake was sent, but no check-in arrived
    59|
    60|Possible causes:
    61|
    62|- Agent process never started or crashed during bootstrap
    63|- Agent ignored startup instructions due to stale templates
    64|- Heartbeat call failed (network/auth/base URL mismatch)
    65|
    66|Actions:
    67|
    68|1. Confirm current templates were synced to gateway.
    69|2. Re-run provisioning/update to trigger a fresh wake.
    70|3. Verify agent can reach Mission Control API and send heartbeat with `X-Agent-Token`.
    71|
    72|### Agent stays provisioning/updating with no retries
    73|
    74|Possible causes:
    75|
    76|- Queue worker not running
    77|- Queue/Redis mismatch between API process and worker process
    78|
    79|Actions:
    80|
    81|1. Verify worker process is running continuously.
    82|2. Verify `rq_redis_url` and `rq_queue_name` are identical for API and worker.
    83|3. Check worker logs for dequeue/handler errors.
    84|
    85|### Agent ended offline quickly
    86|
    87|This is expected when no check-in is received after 3 wake attempts. The system fails fast by design.
    88|
    89|Actions:
    90|
    91|1. Fix check-in path first (startup, network, token, API reachability).
    92|2. Re-run provisioning/update to start a new attempt cycle.
    93|
    94|## Operator Recovery Checklist
    95|
    96|1. Ensure queue worker is running.
    97|2. Sync templates for the gateway.
    98|3. Trigger agent update/provision from Mission Control.
    99|4. Watch logs for:
   100|   - `lifecycle.queue.enqueued`
   101|   - `lifecycle.reconcile.retriggered` (if needed)
   102|   - heartbeat activity / `skip_not_stuck`
   103|5. If still failing, capture:
   104|   - gateway logs around bootstrap
   105|   - worker logs around lifecycle events
   106|   - agent `last_provision_error`, `wake_attempts`, `last_seen_at`
   107|
   108|## Re-syncing auth tokens when Mission Control and OpenClaw have drifted
   109|
   110|Mission Control stores a hash of each agent’s token and provisions OpenClaw by writing templates (e.g. `TOOLS.md`) that include `AUTH_TOKEN`. If the token on the gateway and the backend hash drift (e.g. after a reinstall, token change, or manual edit), heartbeats can fail with 401 and the agent may appear offline.
   111|
   112|To re-sync:
   113|
   114|1. Ensure Mission Control is running (API and queue worker).
   115|2. Run **template sync with token rotation** so the backend issues new agent tokens and rewrites `AUTH_TOKEN` into the gateway’s agent files.
   116|
   117|**Via API (curl):**
   118|
   119|```bash
   120|curl -X POST "http://localhost:8000/api/v1/gateways/GATEWAY_ID/templates/sync?rotate_tokens=true" \
   121|  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_L...KEN"
   122|```
   123|
   124|Replace `GATEWAY_ID` (from the Gateways list or gateway URL in the UI) and `YOUR_LOCAL_AUTH_TOKEN` with your local auth token.
   125|
   126|**Via CLI (from repo root):**
   127|
   128|```bash
   129|cd backend && uv run python scripts/sync_gateway_templates.py --gateway-id GATEWAY_ID --rotate-tokens
   130|```
   131|
   132|After a successful sync, OpenClaw agents will have new `AUTH_TOKEN` values in their workspace files; the next heartbeat or bootstrap will use the new token. If the gateway was offline, trigger a wake/update from Mission Control so agents restart and pick up the new token.
   133|", "total_lines": 132, "file_size": 4938, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

---

COMPLETE SUMMARY: Mission Control (MC) Agent Configuration

WHAT I DID:
Cloned the Roscoe-mission-control repo to /tmp/mission-control and performed a deep review of the backend models, API routes, schemas, services, authentication system, gateway RPC protocol, and documentation.

KEY FINDINGS:

1. DATA MODEL HIERARCHY

The MC data model flows like this:
  Organization -> Gateway -> Board -> Agent -> Task

- Organization: Top-level tenant (has name, UUID)
- Gateway: External OpenClaw runtime endpoint (has URL, token, workspace_root). Belongs to an Organization.
- Board: Work container grouping tasks and agents. Belongs to an Organization AND a Gateway. Has objective, success_metrics, max_agents.
- Agent: Autonomous actor. Belongs to a Gateway and optionally a Board. Has agent_token_hash, heartbeat_config, identity_profile, soul_template, identity_template.
- Task: Work item on a Board. Has status (inbox/in_progress/review/done), priority, assigned_agent_id.

2. AUTHENTICATION SYSTEM

Two layers:
a) Human auth: AUTH_MODE=local uses LOCAL_AUTH_TOKEN (min 50 chars) as a shared bearer token. Users send Authorization: Bearer <LOCAL_AUTH_TOKEN>. AUTH_MODE=clerk uses Clerk JWT.

b) Agent auth: Agents use X-Agent-Token: <agent-token> header. Tokens are auto-generated during provisioning (secrets.token_urlsafe(32)), hashed with PBKDF2-SHA256, and stored as agent_token_hash. Rate-limited to 20 req/60s per IP.

3. API ENDPOINTS (all under /api/v1)

Gateway CRUD:
- GET /api/v1/gateways - List gateways
- POST /api/v1/gateways - Create gateway (name, url, token, workspace_root, allow_insecure_tls, disable_device_pairing)
- GET /api/v1/gateways/{id} - Get gateway
- PATCH /api/v1/gateways/{id} - Update gateway
- DELETE /api/v1/gateways/{id} - Delete gateway
- POST /api/v1/gateways/{id}/templates/sync - Sync templates to agents (with rotate_tokens, reset_sessions flags)

Agent CRUD (admin):
- GET /api/v1/agents - List agents
- POST /api/v1/agents - Create agent (name, board_id, status, heartbeat_config, identity_profile, identity_template, soul_template)
- GET /api/v1/agents/{id} - Get agent
- PATCH /api/v1/agents/{id} - Update agent
- DELETE /api/v1/agents/{id} - Delete agent
- POST /api/v1/agents/{id}/heartbeat - Agent heartbeat
- POST /api/v1/agents/heartbeat - Heartbeat or create (lazy bootstrap)

Agent-scoped routes (agent token auth):
- GET /api/v1/agent/healthz - Agent auth health check
- GET /api/v1/agent/boards - List visible boards
- GET /api/v1/agent/boards/{id}/tasks - List tasks
- POST /api/v1/agent/boards/{id}/tasks - Create task
- PATCH /api/v1/agent/tasks/{id} - Update task
- etc.

Board CRUD:
- POST /api/v1/boards - Create board (name, slug, description, gateway_id required, board_group_id, objective, max_agents)
- GET/PATCH/DELETE /api/v1/boards/{id}

Task CRUD:
- POST /api/v1/tasks - Create task (title, description, status, priority, board_id, assigned_agent_id)
- GET/PATCH /api/v1/tasks/{id}

4. HOW TO REGISTER HERMES AND OPENCLAW

The process to add agents in MC is:

Step 1 - Create a Gateway: A gateway represents an OpenClaw runtime endpoint. MC connects via WebSocket RPC to gateways. The gateway URL should be the WebSocket endpoint of the OpenClaw runtime (e.g., ws://host:18789). When creating a gateway, MC automatically provisions a "main agent" for that gateway.

Step 2 - Create a Board: Every board must be attached to a gateway. Boards represent work containers with objectives and agent limits.

Step 3 - Agents are auto-provisioned: When a board is created and attached to a gateway, MC creates a "board lead" agent. Additional worker agents can be created via the API or by the lead agent itself.

IMPORTANT CONSTRAINT: MC is tightly coupled to the OpenClaw gateway WebSocket RPC protocol. Gateways must implement a specific WS API (methods like health, agents.create, agents.update, sessions.list, wake, send, etc.). MC provisions agents by calling gateway RPC methods over WebSocket. This means:

- Hermes and OpenClaw runtimes must either run an OpenClaw-compatible gateway, OR
- MC must be extended to support alternative runtime protocols

The gateway RPC connects via ws:// or wss:// and uses a JSON-RPC-like protocol with methods including: health, agents.create, agents.update, agents.delete, agents.files.set, sessions.reset, wake, send, chat.send, etc.

5. ENVIRONMENT VARIABLES NEEDED

Root .env:
- POSTGRES_DB, POSTGRES_USER, POSTGRES_PASSWORD, POSTGRES_PORT
- BASE_URL (public backend URL, must be reachable by gateways/agents for heartbeats)
- CORS_ORIGINS
- AUTH_MODE=local
- LOCAL_AUTH_TOKEN (min 50 chars, used for both UI login and API calls)
- FRONTEND_PORT=3000, BACKEND_PORT=8000
- NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL=auto

Backend .env:
- DATABASE_URL=postgresql+psycopg://...
- RQ_REDIS_URL (for background job queue)
- GATEWAY_MIN_VERSION (version compat check)

6. PRACTICAL STEPS FOR AARON

To add Hermes (Railway orchestrator) and OpenClaw (Render workers) as agent runtimes:

a) Log into MC at ops.lawyerincorporated.com
b) Create an Organization (if not done)
c) Create a Gateway for each runtime:
   - "Hermes Gateway" pointing to the OpenClaw gateway URL on Railway
   - "OpenClaw Workers Gateway" pointing to the OpenClaw gateway URL on Render
   Each gateway needs: name, WebSocket URL (ws://...), optional auth token, workspace_root path
d) Create Boards under each gateway with objectives
e) Agents are auto-provisioned (lead + workers)
f) Use POST /api/v1/gateways/{id}/templates/sync to push templates

API example to create a gateway:
```
curl -X POST https://ops.lawyerincorporated.com/api/v1/gateways \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer <LOCAL_AUTH_TOKEN>" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"name":"Hermes Gateway","url":"ws://hermes-host:18789","workspace_root":"/app/workspace","token":"gateway-auth-token"}'
```

CRITICAL CAVEAT: MC expects the gateway URL to be a running OpenClaw gateway with the full WebSocket RPC protocol. If Hermes/OpenClaw runtimes don't run the standard OpenClaw gateway daemon, they cannot be directly registered as MC gateways. The runtimes would need to either:
1. Run the openclaw CLI gateway alongside them, OR
2. Implement a compatible WebSocket RPC shim
